title: China's Core AI Industry Surpasses 1.2 Trillion Yuan; Push to Traditional Sectors Accelerates
detail: Xinhua reported that as of end-2025, China's core AI industry value had exceeded 1.2 trillion yuan (approximately $174 billion), with more than 6,200 AI enterprises operating nationwide. The report emphasized a 2026 push to deepen AI integration into traditional industries and small-to-medium enterprises (SMEs), following the 15th Five-Year Plan's 'AI+ action plan'. A key constraint identified: AI adoption remains concentrated in R&D and marketing functions, with production-line and supply-chain integration still lagging. The State Council's 2026 implementation guidelines direct provincial governments to establish AI adoption quotas for manufacturing enterprises in 10 designated strategic sectors. China's AI market is forecast to exceed 1.73 trillion yuan by 2028. The figures underscore China's position as the world's second-largest AI market by industry value, though the US maintains a substantial lead in frontier model development and AI research investment.

title: Xiong'an 'AI+' Industry Forum Convenes Alongside ZGC Forum — Policy Incentives Unveiled
detail: The Xiong'an 'Artificial Intelligence+' Industry Ecosystem Integration Development Forum was held on April 1 as a parallel event to the 2026 Zhongguancun (ZGC) Forum, drawing more than 400 AI experts, investors, and government officials globally. The event unveiled a comprehensive package of R&D incentives and policy support measures for AI enterprises relocating to or operating in the Xiong'an New Area — the satellite city under construction near Beijing designated under China's 14th and 15th Five-Year Plans as a showcase for AI-integrated urban development. Key announcements included dedicated AI industrial parks, GPU cluster access subsidies for AI startups, and streamlined regulatory pathways for autonomous systems testing. Xiong'an has been designated in the 15th Five-Year Plan as one of China's three national AI demonstration zones alongside Shanghai's Zhangjiang hub and the Shenzhen-Guangzhou-Hong Kong Greater Bay Area tech corridor.

title: Huawei 2025 Annual Results: Revenue Growth Slows to 2.2% as AI Chip Push Faces Transition Headwinds
detail: Huawei released its 2025 annual results on March 31/April 1, revealing a sharp deceleration in top-line growth. Revenue for full-year 2025 reached CNY 880.9 billion (~$127.6 billion), up just 2.2% year-on-year — a dramatic slowdown from 22.4% growth in 2024. Net profit rose 8.7% to CNY 68 billion (~$9.8 billion). R&D investment reached a record CNY 192.3 billion (21.8% of revenue), with the majority directed at Ascend AI chip development and enterprise AI integration. Segment highlights: Intelligent Automotive Solutions surged 72.1%, confirming Huawei's traction as a platform partner for Chinese EV makers; Enterprise business grew modestly; Cloud Computing revenue fell 3.5% to CNY 32.16 billion — the first cloud revenue decline in Huawei's history, reflecting intensified competition from Alibaba Cloud and Tencent Cloud. The slow overall growth reflects a structural reality: Huawei's consumer business (smartphones, PCs) has not fully recovered from US-sanctions-era low points despite the Kirin 9000S revival, while the Ascend AI chip business — though growing — has not yet scaled to replace billions in foregone export revenue. Analysts at Digitimes noted the result suggests AI chip ambitions have yet to drive the double-digit revenue growth seen at US AI infrastructure peers like Nvidia.

title: Shenzhen Activates China's First 10,000-Card Huawei Ascend Cluster — Zero Nvidia Hardware
detail: Shenzhen activated China's first 10,000-card fully domestic AI computing cluster on approximately April 1, using exclusively Huawei Ascend 910C chips, delivering 11,000 petaflops of compute power. Combined with a previously installed 3,000-petaflop cluster at the same Shenzhen facility, the total site capacity reaches 14,000 petaflops — equivalent to approximately 5.5 million personal computers. Nearly 50 organizations signed framework agreements for access to the new cluster; combined booking rate across both phases was 92%. The cluster contains zero Nvidia chips — described as 'full-stack domestically controlled' AI infrastructure. SCMP reported the cluster directly enables DeepSeek V4 training runs on Huawei Ascend hardware, which was simultaneously being prepared for deployment. The activation represents a major milestone in China's 15th Five-Year Plan AI compute infrastructure buildout: for the first time, China has demonstrated that domestically manufactured chips can be deployed at scale sufficient for frontier AI model training, without relying on US or allied semiconductor companies. The Shenzhen cluster's 92% booking rate also signals strong enterprise demand for domestic AI compute — a commercial validation that Chinese companies are willing to pay a premium for hardware independence.
